author: anon-59ff128d
content: Root cause for concurrent test: The threads return Option<FileLock>. When we call handles.into_iter().map(|h| h.join().unwrap()), joining thread 1 might return Some(lock). This lock is held in the iterator chain temporarily, but if the iterator processes lazily or the collect reorders, the lock may drop before all joins complete. More importantly, the real race is: thread A acquires lock, thread A's function returns (lock moves to JoinHandle), threads B-G fail, thread H hasn't run yet. Main thread starts joining: joins A (gets Some(lock)), lock is now in results vec. But H may not have tried yet. When H runs, the lock is held by results vec, so H should fail. But if the barrier releases all 8 threads and thread A returns very quickly, its JoinHandle is ready. If the main thread isn't waiting on the barrier but is already in the collect() loop, it could join A, get the lock, then the lock is in the vec, then join B-H. This should still work. The ACTUAL issue: the results vec keeps all Option<FileLock> alive until the assertion. So the lock IS held. The 2-winners scenario means create_new somehow succeeded twice. This is a bug in the stale-lock cleanup: both threads see the lock file from the winner, check is_lock_stale, and since the PID matches the current process (same test process), is_lock_stale returns false. So neither should clean it up. Both should fail on create_new. Unless... thread 1 acquires, its lock is in the return value (moved to JoinHandle), and before thread 2 runs, thread 1's JoinHandle gets collected — which moves the FileLock into the results vec (not dropped). Hmm. Actually I think the issue is timing: thread 1 acquires the lock, returns. The lock is now in the JoinHandle. Thread 2 tries acquire: lock file exists, is_lock_stale checks PID (same process, alive) → not stale → create_new fails → WouldBlock → returns None. This is correct. But with 8 threads on a busy CI, there could be significant scheduling delays. If thread 1 returns, and by the time the main thread calls join on thread 1's handle, the FileLock is extracted from the JoinHandle — this is fine, it's moved into results, not dropped. The fix: use a shared Mutex to collect the locks, keeping them alive for the duration of the test.
